title: Introduction to Programming – In-Depth Object Orientation Quiz

explanation.`Mammal`: Correct. The generic type of `animalList` is set to `Mammal`, so only objects of type `Mammal` and its subclasses can be added to it.
explanation.`Bear`: Correct. The generic type of `animalList` is set to `Mammal`, so only objects of type `Mammal` and its subclasses can be added to it. `Bear` is a subclass of `Mammal`.
explanation.`Animal`: Incorrect. The generic type of `animalList` is set to `Mammal`, so only objects of type `Mammal` and its subclasses can be added to it. `Animal` is a superclass of `Mammal`.
explanation.`Bird`: Incorrect. The generic type of `animalList` is set to `Mammal`, so only objects of type `Mammal` and its subclasses can be added to it. `Bird` is not a subclass of `Mammal`.

questions.source_reference: W07 In-Depth Object Orientation – Generics (List<T>, generic type bounds, subclass compatibility)

options: `Aurora is 16 years old!` will be printed to the console.
At the end of the `main()` method, there are 4 entries in the Map `disneyPrincessesAge`.
`Aurora is 14 years old!` will be printed to the console.
`Aurora is 30 years old!` will be printed to the console.
questions.correct_answer: `Aurora is 16 years old!` will be printed to the console.

explanation.`Aurora is 16 years old!` will be printe: Correct. When `map.put(key, value)` is called on a Map and the key already exists, the new value overwrites the existing one. The value associated with `"Aurora"` is overwritten with `16` the second time it is added.
explanation.At the end of the `main()` method, there: Incorrect. When `map.put(key, value)` is called and the key already exists, the existing value is overwritten — no second entry with the same key is added. The map ends up with 3 entries (`Aurora → 16`, `Ariel → 16`, `Rapunzel → 18`), not 4.
explanation.`Aurora is 14 years old!` will be printe: Incorrect. When the same key is added to a Map twice, the second value overwrites the first. The original entry `14` is replaced by `16`.
explanation.`Aurora is 30 years old!` will be printe: Incorrect. When `map.put(key, value)` is called and the key already exists, the new value replaces the old one — the values are not summed. `Aurora`'s age is `16`, not `14 + 16 = 30`.

questions.source_reference: W07 In-Depth Object Orientation – Generics (Map<K,V>, HashMap, put() overwrite behaviour, get())

options: There is a compile error because `throws EmptyException` is missing at the definition of the method `removePositives()`.
There is a compile error because `removePositives()` is not called in a `try-catch` block.
There is a compile error because the `HashMap` accepts `<Integer, Integer>`, but receives a pair of `<int, int>`.

explanation.There is a compile error because `throws: Incorrect. `EmptyException` is an unchecked exception because it extends `RuntimeException`, the superclass of all exceptions that can be thrown during normal program execution. Unchecked exceptions do not need to be declared with `throws`.
explanation.There is a compile error because `remove: Incorrect. `EmptyException` is an unchecked exception because it extends `RuntimeException`. Unchecked exceptions do not need to be caught, so calling the method outside of a `try-catch` block is perfectly legal.
Correct. The array passed to `removePositives()` contains only positive integers, so no element is ever added to the `HashMap`. When the method checks `res.isEmpty()`, the condition is `true` and `EmptyException` is thrown.
explanation.There is a compile error because the `Ha: Incorrect. `Integer` is the wrapper class of `int`. Java automatically converts between `int` and `Integer` through a mechanism called autoboxing, so passing `int` values where `Integer` is expected compiles without error.

questions.source_reference: W07 In-Depth Object Orientation – Generics (Map<K,V>, HashMap); Exceptions (unchecked exceptions, RuntimeException, autoboxing)

learning_objectives: Apply generic type bounds to determine which object types are compatible with a parameterised collection
Understand Map.put() overwrite behaviour and correctly predict the state of a HashMap after duplicate key insertions
Distinguish checked from unchecked exceptions, and understand that RuntimeException subclasses require neither a throws declaration nor a try-catch block; recognise autoboxing between int and Integer
